name: KL Debugger Codex v2 (ST + Lumiverse Surface + Backend)
description: Retrieval-oriented debugger lorebook for SillyTavern macro semantics, Lumiverse surface macros and backend behavior, ST↔Lumiverse variable bridging, and forensic diagnostics for artifacts, regex, pipeline collisions, and state desync.

1.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when someone is unsure whether the macro shape itself is valid.

Documented behavior:
- ST macros are enclosed in double curly braces.
- Macro names are case-insensitive.
- Macros are used throughout prompts, character cards, lorebooks, Quick Replies, and other macro-supporting text fields.

Debugger guidance:
- Confirm the field actually supports macros before blaming the braces.
- Do not treat capitalization differences as a bug.
- A valid-looking macro can still fail if the field never evaluates it.
1.name: ST macro basics and case-insensitive names

2.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when argument syntax is being questioned.

Documented behavior:
- Single-argument macros may use a space separator.
- Multi-argument macros use ::.
- Single-colon syntax exists as legacy syntax but is not recommended for new content.

Debugger guidance:
- Do not convert valid space or :: syntax into legacy single-colon syntax as a first fix.
- If an example works only after drastic separator rewriting, suspect a different layer first.
2.name: ST macro argument separators

3.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when spacing inside a macro is being blamed for a failure.

Documented behavior:
- Whitespace around the macro name, separators, and arguments is ignored.
- Spaced and compact forms are equivalent if the surrounding field actually evaluates macros.

Debugger guidance:
- Do not strip spaces as the first fix.
- Test a trivial built-in macro in the same field.
- If simple built-ins also fail, suspect field support, escaping, or preprocessing order instead of spacing.
3.name: ST macro whitespace ignored

4.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when one macro is built from another macro.

Documented behavior:
- Nested macros are supported.
- The innermost macro resolves first.
- Its output becomes part of the outer macro input.

Debugger guidance:
- Debug the inner macro first.
- Compare the outer macro against a hardcoded expanded value.
- If the hardcoded version works but the nested one does not, the inner result or field support is usually the real problem.
4.name: ST nested macros resolve inner-first

5.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when multiline macro content loses leading or trailing whitespace or shifts left.

Documented behavior:
- Scoped syntax passes the block content as the last argument.
- Scoped content is trimmed by default.
- Consistent indentation is dedented by default.

Debugger guidance:
- Do not diagnose missing indentation as corruption immediately.
- Check whether the block was intentionally using default scoped behavior.
- Use preserve-whitespace mode only when exact formatting must survive unchanged.
5.name: ST scoped macros trim and dedent by default

6.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when exact indentation, blank lines, or leading and trailing whitespace matters.

Documented behavior:
- The # flag disables the default trim and dedent behavior for scoped content.
- This is the correct mode for code blocks, aligned text, and formatting-sensitive payloads.

Debugger guidance:
- If a scoped entry stores code, markdown, templates, or preformatted text, check whether the opening tag uses the preserve-whitespace flag.
- Do not recommend this mode when normalized text is actually desired.
6.name: ST # flag preserves scoped whitespace exactly

7.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when raw macro-looking text appears intentionally or accidentally in output.

Documented behavior:
- Escaped double curly braces are rendered literally instead of being evaluated as macros.

Debugger guidance:
- Inspect the raw stored text for escape characters before blaming syntax.
- A copied code sample or serializer may have introduced escapes.
- If literal output was intended, keep the escape. If evaluation was intended, remove it and retest in the same field.
7.name: ST macro escaping outputs literal braces

8.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern variables

Use this when shorthand variable syntax is being mistaken for malformed macro text.

Documented behavior:
- A leading dot refers to local variable shorthand.
- A leading dollar sign refers to global variable shorthand.
- These markers are scope prefixes, not part of the variable name.

Debugger guidance:
- Translate shorthand mentally into its local or global meaning before diagnosing.
- Investigate scope confusion before rewriting syntax.
- If the same value is expected across chats or sessions, verify whether global scope was really intended.
8.name: ST variable shorthand uses . local and $ global

9.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern variables

Use this when shorthand assignment or fallback syntax is being misread.

Documented behavior:
- Variable shorthand supports get, set, increment, decrement, add, subtract, fallback, assign-if-missing, and comparison operators.
- Whitespace around shorthand operators is allowed.
- Nested macros can appear inside shorthand values.

Debugger guidance:
- Do not assume a shorthand entry is plain text just because it contains operators.
- If the user expects a value to persist, diagnose scope first, then operator behavior.
9.name: ST variable shorthand operators

10.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern conditionals

Use this when an if block behaves differently than expected.

Documented behavior:
- {{if}} renders content conditionally.
- Conditions may use macro names, nested macros, variable shorthand, or literal text.
- Falsy values include empty string, false, 0, off, and no.
- ! inverts the condition, and {{else}} provides an alternative branch.

Debugger guidance:
- Inspect the actual resolved value of the condition instead of arguing from the raw source.
- Check whether the issue is truthiness, not syntax.
10.name: ST conditional macros and truthiness

11.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: SillyTavern macro syntax

Use this when inline notes appear to vanish or scoped comment blocks do not reach the model.

Documented behavior:
- The comment macro removes its content from output.
- Comments can be inline or scoped.

Debugger guidance:
- Do not diagnose a missing note as accidental loss if it was wrapped in a comment macro.
- Check whether a supposed instruction was actually commented out.
11.name: ST macro comments are removed from output

key: backend set variable but prompt can't see it
12.content:
TYPE: bridge
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse local variables and prompt-side local variable reads

Use this when a value written in Lumiverse is expected to appear through prompt-side variable access.

Documented bridge:
- Lumiverse local variables are chat-scoped.
- They correspond to the same local variable storage model that prompt-side getvar and setvar read from.

Debugger guidance:
- Verify the chat identity used for the write.
- Confirm the write and the prompt render belong to the same chat context.
- Investigate timing and scope before blaming prompt syntax.
keys: backend set variable but prompt can't see it
12.name: Lumiverse local variables bridge to ST getvar/setvar behavior

13.content:
TYPE: bridge
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse global storage versus prompt-side global-looking mechanisms

Use this when someone assumes every global-sounding system is the same store.

Documented bridge:
- Lumiverse global variables persist across chats.
- That does not mean every prompt-side global mechanism is the same underlying storage.

Debugger guidance:
- Identify exactly which global system the write used.
- Do not collapse similarly named scopes into one mental bucket.
- Check whether the problem is absence of data or a mismatch between two different stores.
13.name: Lumiverse global variables are separate from per-chat global-looking prompt storage

14.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse user-facing macros

Use this when someone is reasoning about Lumiverse macro surface syntax.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse macros are written as template variables like {{macro_name}}.
- They are replaced with dynamic content during prompt assembly.
- Preset blocks are the primary use case.

Debugger guidance:
- Distinguish surface macro syntax from backend extension macros.
- If the macro text looks correct but resolves unexpectedly, inspect execution location and timing before rewriting syntax.
14.name: Lumiverse macro basics and prompt-assembly timing

15.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse World Books

Use this when someone expects Lumiverse macros inside World Book entry content to resolve dynamically.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse lists World Book entries among places where macros are relevant.
- It explicitly notes that World Book entries are not macro-evaluated in Lumiverse.

Debugger guidance:
- Keep World Book entry content as plain guidance and retrieval text.
- Do not rely on live Lumiverse macros inside the entry body.
- If dynamic macro behavior is required, move it to a macro-evaluated surface such as preset blocks, guided generation content, Author's Note, or chat messages.
15.name: Lumiverse World Book entries are not macro-evaluated

16.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when the problem involves names or character profile fields.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents identity macros such as user, char, and group.
- It also documents character macros such as description, personality, and scenario.

Debugger guidance:
- Separate identity resolution bugs from backend data bugs.
- If a character field appears blank, inspect the underlying character data before inventing macro syntax fixes.
16.name: Lumiverse identity and character macro families

17.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when the prompt depends on message history or chat counters.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents chat-state macros such as lastMessage, messageCount, and messageAt::0.

Debugger guidance:
- Treat chat-state macros as prompt-assembly reads of current chat data.
- If a count or message lookup looks wrong, verify which chat and message position is actually being referenced.
17.name: Lumiverse chat-state macros

18.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when a Lumiverse prompt uses variable-style macros directly.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents variable macros including getvar-style syntax and shorthand forms.
- Examples include {{getvar::key}}, {{.var}}, and {{$globalVar}}.

Debugger guidance:
- Diagnose scope and data source before accusing the syntax.
- If the same value is expected in multiple places, check which scope is actually being read.
18.name: Lumiverse variable macro family

19.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when a Lumiverse prompt branches conditionally.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents conditional syntax such as {{if .var == 5}}...{{else}}...{{/if}}.

Debugger guidance:
- Inspect the resolved condition and scope of the referenced variable.
- If a branch is wrong, treat it as a data or truthiness problem before treating it as a formatting problem.
19.name: Lumiverse conditional macros

20.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when a prompt uses helper macros outside simple identity or variable reads.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents string macros such as upper, replace, len, and split.
- It documents math macros such as calc, clamp, min, and max.
- It documents logic macros such as switch, default, and, and not.
- It documents random macros such as random, pick, and roll.
- It documents formatting macros such as bullets and numbered.

Debugger guidance:
- Identify which helper family is being used before diagnosing.
- If output shape is wrong, inspect the helper macro family rather than assuming a general rendering bug.
20.name: Lumiverse string, math, logic, random, and formatting macro families

21.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse built-in macro categories

Use this when a prompt references Council or Lumia-specific helpers.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse documents Council and Lumia macro examples such as lumiaCouncilDeliberation and loomStyle.

Debugger guidance:
- Treat these as specialized built-ins, not generic text placeholders.
- If they fail, verify the relevant Council or Lumia context before blaming general macro parsing.
21.name: Lumiverse Council and Lumia macro family

22.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse custom macros

Use this when a macro value is already available before generation begins.

Documented behavior:
- The push model is preferred for values the extension already knows.
- The extension updates the host with updateMacroValue.
- The host can then use the latest pushed value without generation-time lookup work.

Debugger guidance:
- Treat freshness as an explicit extension responsibility.
- If the value already exists in cache, a listener, or a polling loop, push it proactively instead of waiting until generation.
22.name: Lumiverse push model is the preferred custom macro pattern

23.content:
TYPE: diagnostic
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse custom macros

Use this when a custom macro works but slows prompt assembly or generation.

Documented behavior:
- If no pushed value exists, the host can fall back to calling the macro handler at generation time.
- That path adds latency and can wait several seconds per macro.

Debugger guidance:
- Search for updateMacroValue usage first.
- Inspect whether the handler is doing network work or slow computation during generation.
- Reserve pull behavior for values that genuinely depend on generation-time context.
23.name: Lumiverse pull fallback adds generation-time latency

24.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse observability

Use this when log attribution is muddy.

Documented behavior:
- Lumiverse extension logs are prefixed with the Spindle identifier.

Debugger guidance:
- Treat prefixed logs as the cleanest source of extension truth.
- Distinguish click events, UI changes, and speculative state from confirmed logged transitions.
- When a bug is ambiguous, prefer the path that can be tied to authoritative prefixed logs.
24.name: Lumiverse logs are authoritative when they carry the Spindle prefix

25.content:
TYPE: diagnostic
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse frontend and backend messaging

Use this when UI actions fire but backend behavior, user scoping, or visible updates do not line up.

Documented behavior:
- Frontend messages are JSON-serializable payloads.
- Incoming frontend messages include a user context.
- Routing by a stable type field is the standard pattern.

Debugger guidance:
- Log both the payload and the user context at send and receipt.
- Verify that sender and receiver agree on the same type contract.
- Separate optimistic UI changes from confirmed backend commits.
25.name: Lumiverse frontend routing depends on payload shape and user context

26.content:
TYPE: reference
AUTHORITY: documented implementation behavior
SCOPE: Lumiverse prompt mutation pipeline

Use this when the assembled prompt looked correct but the provider still received altered messages.

Documented behavior:
- Interceptors run after prompt assembly and before provider send.
- They can modify, add, or remove messages.
- Priority order matters.
- Slow interceptors can be skipped.

Debugger guidance:
- Compare the message array before and after interceptor execution.
- Inspect ordering and timing before blaming model behavior.
- Treat interceptors as the final mutation layer, not as passive observers.
26.name: Lumiverse interceptors mutate the final message pipeline

27.content:
TYPE: forensic pattern
AUTHORITY: working theory from recurring debugger cases
SCOPE: Shared macro debugging

Use this when macro-shaped text survives into final output unchanged.

Likely interpretation:
- The field never evaluated macros.
- The text was escaped or serialized before macro resolution.
- Another transformation layer treated the content as inert text.

Debugger guidance:
- Test one tiny built-in macro in the same field.
- Compare raw source text against postprocessed text.
- Only start rewriting syntax after field support and escaping are ruled out.
27.name: Raw macro text surviving into output usually means layer failure, not syntax failure

28.content:
TYPE: house rule
AUTHORITY: project-stability pattern
SCOPE: Artifact UI structure

Use this when an artifact renders once, then breaks when the view, state, or tab changes.

Likely interpretation:
- More than one container is acting like the surface root.
- Alternate states are rebuilding the shell instead of changing content inside a stable frame.
- The DOM is technically valid but structurally hostile to stateful updates.

Debugger guidance:
- Count true top-level shells.
- Keep one surface, one root, one stable frame.
- Fix structure before chasing styling symptoms.
28.name: Duplicate roots or competing shells destabilize stateful artifacts

29.content:
TYPE: house rule
AUTHORITY: project-stability pattern
SCOPE: Artifact UI structure

Use this when the default view is fine but alternative views look half-broken, duplicated, or misaligned.

Likely interpretation:
- Each state generates a different shell instead of reusing one durable frame.
- Persistent controls are duplicated.
- Selectors or behaviors assume elements that vanish between states.

Debugger guidance:
- Compare the shell across states instead of only checking the changed content.
- Keep the frame stable and swap content inside it.
- Treat mode changes as content transitions, not artifact rewrites.
29.name: State-switch collisions happen when structure changes instead of content

30.content:
TYPE: forensic pattern
AUTHORITY: working theory from recurring debugger cases
SCOPE: Regex and postprocessing

Use this when cleanup or replacement logic suddenly removes much more than intended.

Likely interpretation:
- The pattern is matching across tag boundaries or nested blocks.
- Greedy or broad classes are swallowing markup structure.
- A replacement is running repeatedly on already modified content.

Debugger guidance:
- Test the pattern on a minimal HTML-rich sample, not plain text only.
- Compare raw output to postprocessed output.
- Narrow the scope before tuning the replacement text.
30.name: Broad regex becomes dangerous on HTML-rich content

31.content:
TYPE: forensic pattern
AUTHORITY: working theory from recurring debugger cases
SCOPE: Postprocessing pipeline

Use this when the raw generation looks fine but the final render is broken.

Likely interpretation:
- Two or more mutation stages target overlapping content.
- Cleanup runs after a formatting step that expected its own structure to survive.
- A plain-text assumption is colliding with already-inserted markup.

Debugger guidance:
- Inspect each stage separately.
- Disable one pass at a time.
- Order broad cleanup before delicate formatting when possible.
31.name: Multiple mutation passes can make correct output look broken

32.content:
TYPE: forensic pattern
AUTHORITY: working theory from recurring debugger cases
SCOPE: Lumiverse pushed macro freshness

Use this when a macro resolves instantly but returns yesterday's truth.

Likely interpretation:
- The push path exists, but not every relevant state transition triggers a refresh.
- One event updates the cache while another event path does not.
- The extension assumes freshness will happen implicitly.

Debugger guidance:
- Map every event that should trigger an update.
- Compare fresh cases to stale cases and look for the missing transition.
- Log both source-state changes and push timing.
32.name: Pushed macro values can be fast and still stale

key: button works visually but not really
33.content:
TYPE: forensic pattern
AUTHORITY: working theory from recurring debugger cases
SCOPE: Frontend and backend state reconciliation

Use this when the UI appears to react immediately, but refresh or later behavior reveals the backend never committed the same state.

Likely interpretation:
- The frontend is showing optimistic state.
- The backend confirmation path failed or never reconciled.
- Transport worked in one direction but not the other.

Debugger guidance:
- Distinguish speculative UI state from confirmed backend state.
- Log commit points, not just click points.
- Reconcile visual state from backend truth after writes.
keys: button works visually but not really
33.name: A responsive UI can still be desynced from backend truth

34.content:
TYPE: house rule
AUTHORITY: project-stability policy
SCOPE: Shared debugger policy

Use this when content passes syntax checks and still behaves badly inside the real project.

Core principle:
- Platform-valid is not automatically project-stable.
- A project may rely on stricter conventions such as one root per surface, stable shells across states, limited mutation layers, explicit routing fields, or tightly scoped regex cleanup.

Debugger guidance:
- Name the violated project rule explicitly.
- Do not present a house rule as if the engine documentation mandated it.
- Prefer stable patterns used by known-good artifacts over merely valid output.
34.name: Technically valid output can still violate project stability rules
